prompt: Is it ever ethical to lie to someone you love? Answer in prose, taking a position.
response:
Yes—there are moments when a lie told to someone you love is not only ethical but an act of love itself. The moral weight of a statement is measured not only by its factual accuracy but by the care it shows for the person who hears it. When the truth would serve no purpose except to wound, and when the wound would be gratuitous—when it would not help the beloved grow, protect them from danger, or allow them to make a better choice—then silence or even a gentle untruth can be the more compassionate path.

Imagine a partner in the last stages of a terminal illness who asks, “Am I going to die tonight?” The literal answer may be yes, but the loving answer might be, “I’m right here, and we still have time.” That sentence is not a denial of death; it is a refusal to let fear eclipse the small, remaining light. The lie does not manipulate or coerce; it shields a fragile heart from a pain that cannot be avoided but need not be sharpened.

Or consider the parent who, during a time of financial ruin, tells a child that everything will be all right. The parent is not promising miracles; they are preserving the child’s sense of safety long enough for solutions to be found. The lie is temporary scaffolding, erected so that the structure of the child’s world does not collapse before it can be rebuilt.

These are not lies told to escape accountability or to control another’s choices; they are lies told to carry someone across a chasm they cannot yet bridge on their own. They are ethical precisely because they place the beloved’s well-being above the speaker’s moral comfort. The lover who insists on “total honesty” at every turn often mistakes rigidity for integrity. Integrity, in love, is judged by whether our words and silences finally serve the flourishing of the person we cherish. Sometimes that flourishing requires the shelter of a merciful fiction, offered not as deception but as a gift of time, of peace, of hope just large enough to survive the night.
